rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit via the parent statement: “Towards satisfactory water management … covering … flood risk reduction … and water quality protection,” which is an adaptation framing. The policy action supports this by maintaining aquatic water quality through “constant monitoring … and an intervention procedure” and by working “to reduce the discharge of sewage into the canal and the Seine river.” Although no explicit adaptation token appears in the sub-text (gate failure), the Stage 3c verdict is `explicit_parent`, so the row should be retained under Adaptation.
policy_statement: Maintain good water quality in ponds and semi-natural water areas via constant monitoring and a defined/applied intervention procedure; and work with other authorities to reduce sewage discharges into the canal and the Seine river.
mechanism_description: The city will carry out constant monitoring of ponds and semi-natural water areas, define and apply an intervention procedure, and work with other authorities to reduce sewage discharges into the canal and river to maintain good water quality.
primary_causal_pathway: Water management operations → ↓ pollutant loads / ↑ water quality → ↓ climate-related vulnerability of aquatic systems and dependent services
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human/system change: The city implements continuous water-quality monitoring, sets trigger thresholds and response procedures, and coordinates with other authorities to reduce sewage discharges. (2) Mediating process: Reduced nutrient/pathogen/organic pollution lowers eutrophication, oxygen depletion, and contaminant spikes—problems often exacerbated by heat, low flows, and intense rainfall/CSO events. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased robustness of aquatic ecosystems and water services under climate stress, reducing ecological damage and potential public-health/service disruptions during climate extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is maintaining healthier aquatic ecosystems and water bodies that sustain climate resilience (e.g., better water quality during heat/drought and fewer ecological stress impacts).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: operational water monitoring + pollution-control actions lead to improved water quality, which primarily reduces vulnerability of ecosystems/services to climate-amplified stressors (heat, drought, extreme rainfall).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be small mitigation co-benefits (e.g., less organic load reducing downstream methane), the main intended and largest effect is safeguarding water system function and health—an adaptation/resilience outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the same monitoring/intervention were explicitly framed around carbon sequestration in aquatic systems, it could lean Nature-Based Solutions; or if it targeted energy use in treatment plants, Resource Efficiency. Those are not the dominant pathway here.  
4) Applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: Continuous monitoring + intervention to prevent sewage/pollution impacts consistently functions as resilience protection of aquatic water quality under variable/extreme conditions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., public health, biodiversity, flood/overflow management) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Water-quality protection can be classified as Nature-Based Solutions if the mechanism is primarily ecosystem restoration/blue-green habitat expansion; here it is primarily monitoring, discharge reduction, and operational control, so Adaptation is dominant.
key_indicators: "constant monitoring will be carried out"; "an intervention procedure will be defined and applied"; "undertakes to do its utmost"; "in cooperation with the Regional Council and the municipalities"; "reduce the discharge of sewage into the canal and the Seine river"
co_benefits: Improved public health and recreation; biodiversity/ecosystem enhancement; general water pollution control (non-climate); compliance with water-quality objectives
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be read partly as a regulatory/enforcement or infrastructure measure to reduce sewage discharges, but the excerpt does not specify binding limits, permitting/enforcement tools, or concrete investment actions—so it is classified as a planning/operational strategy.
